proposed_short: Using the future to guess about the present (Saranno le tre = it's probably three), or the futuro anteriore to guess about the past (Avrà perso il treno = he's probably missed it).
proposed_long: Italian uses the future tense for more than time to come: it also expresses a SUPPOSITION about the present. 'Saranno le tre' does not mean 'it will be three' but 'it's probably three'; 'Avrà vent'anni' means 'he's about twenty'. The compound futuro anteriore does the same for a completed past: 'Avrà perso il treno' = 'he's probably missed the train'. English has no dedicated tense for this and falls back on 'must', 'probably' or 'I suppose'. Grammars call this the futuro epistemico or futuro di probabilità (suppositional / epistemic future); it is the reason a bare future form in Italian can, in context, be a guess rather than a prediction.
rationale: Used throughout the future-usage probability explanations. The glossary has 'future' (the form) but no entry for this specific epistemic FUNCTION, which is the headline usage gap the wave-2 dispatch fills and has no English-tense equivalent to lean on.

proposed_short: A subordinate clause of time introduced by quando, appena, finché, dopo che; in Italian it takes the future for a still-future action, where English uses the present.
proposed_long: A temporal subordinate clause locates the main action in time relative to another event, introduced by a temporal conjunction such as quando (when), appena (as soon as), finché (until / as long as) or dopo che (after). Italian and English diverge on the tense inside it: for an action still to come, Italian keeps the future ('Quando arriverai, ti chiamerò'), whereas English switches the subordinate verb to the present ('When you arrive, I'll call you'). Carrying the English present into Italian ('quando arrivi...') is one of the most persistent B1 errors, which is why the future-in-temporal-subordinate is taught as a function in its own right.
rationale: Used in the temporal-subordinate usage explanations. The glossary has no entry for this clause type, which is the whole subject of one of the three new usage leaves.
